title: Capitalism's Climate
html:
TL;DR

  • WR121 essay (2,093 words, revised down from 2,999) submitted under Ms. St. John at OSU December 2021 — argues capitalism's profit-incentive is the root cause of the sixth mass extinction, not capitalism-the-system itself
  • Cites: 100 companies → 70% of greenhouse gases over 20yr; 139 US Congress members denying climate change; ExxonMobil's $23M to climate-denial research; the Koch brothers as the prototypical capitalist-puppeteers
  • Anti-thesis case: Cuba under Castro declared the only nation meeting WWF/Global-Footprint-Network sustainability minimums (2006) — used as the proof-of-concept that incentive structure shapes climate outcome
  • Quotes Marx's Das Kapital line that "capitalist production disturbs the metabolic interaction between man and the earth," Angela Davis on capitalist democracy foreclosing radical equality, and Cambridge dictionary definitions of both terms
  • The Jack Schultz byline at the top is suspicious — likely a header copy-paste error or a peer-review attribution; the body is unambiguously Yahir's voice and concerns

Notes

A college freshman year argumentative essay for Writing 121 that already contains the seeds of Yahir's later surveillance state / mindless human interface disruption / social control thinking — namely, that incentive design > stated values, and that systems get the outcomes they pay for.

The structural move is interesting: rather than argue for communism, Yahir argues that capitalism as a mindset (greatest-personal-profit-as-virtue) is the cancer, while leaving the door open that a capitalist society with different incentives could in principle do better. The Cuba/Castro section functions as the existence-proof — a small island under economic siege accidentally became sustainable because the incentive structure pointed at communal wellbeing instead of profit.

Lineage: this is the first vault appearance of Yahir engaging seriously with Marx, and the climate-incentive lens reappears in 2022 10 11 happiness dystopia (where the perversion is loneliness-points instead of carbon-emissions) and in his later product-design instincts about incentive alignment.

Citations cited: Christina Nunez (Nat Geo), Letícia Casado & Ernesto Londoño (NYT on Bolsonaro), Ngan Le on fast fashion / Zara / Forever 21 / polyester microplastics, Patricia Lane on Cuba.
